--- Page 1 ---
Reconfigurable Earth Observation Satellite Scheduling Problem
Brycen D. PearlвҲ—, Joseph M. MillerвҖ , and Hang Woon LeeвҖЎ
West Virginia University, Morgantown, WV, 26506
Earth observation satellites (EOSs) play a pivotal role in capturing and analyzing planetary
phenomena, ranging from natural disasters to societal development. The EOS scheduling
problem (EOSSP), which optimizes the schedule of EOSs, is often solved with respect to nadir-
directional EOS systems, thus restricting the observation time of targets and, consequently, the
effectiveness of each EOS. This paper leverages state-of-the-art constellation reconfigurability to
develop the reconfigurable EOS scheduling problem (REOSSP), wherein EOSs are assumed to
be maneuverable, forming a more optimal constellation configuration at multiple opportunities
during a schedule. This paper develops a novel mixed-integer linear programming formulation
for the REOSSP to optimally solve the scheduling problem for given parameters. Additionally,
since the REOSSP can be computationally expensive for large-scale problems, a rolling
horizon procedure (RHP) solution method is developed. The performance of the REOSSP is
benchmarked against the EOSSP, which serves as a baseline, through a set of random instances
where problem characteristics are varied and a case study in which Hurricane Sandy is used to
demonstrate realistic performance. These experiments demonstrate the value of constellation
reconfigurability in its application to the EOSSP, yielding solutions that improve performance,
while the RHP enhances computational runtime for large-scale REOSSP instances.
Nomenclature
рқҗөvarious
=
battery-related values
рқҗ¶
=
arbitrary weight of data downlink in objective functions
рқ‘җ
=
cost of orbital maneuver
рқ‘җmax
=
budget for total orbital maneuver costs
рқҗ·various
=
data-related values
рқҗә
=
number of ground stations
This paper is a substantially revised version of the paper AIAA 2025-0589, presented at the 2025 AIAA SciTech Forum, Orlando, FL, January
6-10, 2025. It offers new results, an additional solution methodology, and a better description of the materials.
вҲ—Ph.D. Student, Department of Mechanical, Materials and Aerospace Engineering, Student Member AIAA.
вҖ Undergraduate Student, Department of Mechanical, Materials and Aerospace Engineering.
вҖЎAssistant Professor, Department of Mechanical, Materials and Aerospace Engineering; hangwoon.lee@mail.wvu.edu. Member AIAA
(Corresponding Author).
arXiv:2507.10394v3  [math.OC]  7 Nov 2025

I. Introduction
S
atellite systems are crucial for gathering information on various planetary phenomena on Earth through Earth
observation (EO), leveraging remote sensing to provide observations in the form of radio frequencies, radar, lidar,
and optical imaging, among other measurements. EO data may be used to benefit studies of direct societal activity,
such as in use observing agricultural drought progression [1], providing additional security and situational awareness
through maritime surveillance [2], and providing insight into current and potential civil infrastructure development [3].
Additionally, EO data may be used to provide necessary information regarding natural processes, such as when utilized
to monitor and report on natural disasters [4] and in use modeling the potential spread of diseases [5].
Optimally collecting and providing EO data requires considering the Earth observation satellite scheduling problem
(EOSSP), an optimization problem where each satellite task is treated as a decision variable to maximize observation
rewards. Typically, formulations of the EOSSP include the scheduling of observation tasks to gather data, data downlink
tasks to transmit data to ground stations, and physical or operational constraints [6вҖ“8]. An important constraint of
many EOSSP formulations is the restriction of observation tasks only to times when targets are visible to satellites [9],
defined as the visible time window (VTW) [10]. As a result, the amount of data collected through observation is directly
influenced by the VTW, further underlining the VTW as one of the most impactful aspects of any EOSSP.
The VTW, and consequently the maximum amount of collectible data, are maintained as fixed parameters due to
the underlying assumptions present in EOSSP formulations. Two such underlying assumptions are the restriction of
the pointing direction of satellites, employing only nadir-directional observations that present a fixed VTW, and the
restriction to a given orbit that is incapable of being manipulated [11]. Such limitations imply that the VTW is a fixed
parameter of the EOSSP, thus reducing the potential target observations or data downlink through the restricted VTW
access to targets and ground stations (when considered), respectively. The limitations can be alleviated using current
state-of-the-art satellite concepts of operations (CONOPS), allowing the VTW to be manipulated as an intermediate
decision variable [12].
One such state-of-the-art satellite CONOPS is satellite agility, a key aspect of the agile Earth observation satellite
scheduling problem (AEOSSP). Satellite agility refers to the ability of satellites to perform attitude control (slewing) in
roll, pitch, and/or yaw [12]. The AEOSSP leverages slewing as an additional task, allowing the satellites to change their
pointing angle beyond the nadir direction, thus extending existing VTWs or creating new VTWs that were previously
beyond the nadir directional swath width [9]. Prominent formulations of the AEOSSP include the use of slewing to
maximize the number or profit of observations under various operational conditions [8, 9, 13, 14] and the use of slewing
to prioritize the amount of data returned to a ground station or operator [7, 11, 15]. However, satellite agility suffers from
3


--- Page 4 ---
a degradation in observation quality due to the optical tilt and resolution change that results from angled observations,
as a large slewing angle causes a difference in the look angle compared to nadir-directional observations [16].
An additional state-of-the-art satellite CONOPS that has not yet been implemented as an extension to the EOSSP
is constellation reconfigurability. Constellation reconfigurability is defined as the capability of satellites within a
constellation to perform orbital maneuvers to reform the constellation into a more optimal state [17, 18]. Such a capability
provides flexibility and responsiveness to satellite systems [19, 20], potentially allowing new opportunities for tasks that
were previously unavailable. General uses of constellation reconfigurability external to EO include telecommunications
systems with staged satellite deployment for the minimization of costs [17, 21, 22], as well as response to incapacitated
assets to compensate for performance losses [23]. Investigations of constellation reconfigurability within EO include
single-stage [18, 24, 25] and multi-stage [26вҖ“28] reconfiguration for natural disaster impact monitoring, reconfiguration
between selected operation modes (regional and global observation) [19, 29], and altitude changes both for observations
of earthquake impacts [30] and tracking mobile targets [31]. Within their investigations, Refs. [22, 30, 31] make use
of low-thrust maneuvering, which, while valuable for fuel-efficient constellation reconfiguration, is computationally
expensive and may not be as effective as high-thrust maneuvering in highly dynamic environments. Furthermore, previous
research in Refs. [32, 33] provides a comparative analysis between nadir-directional, agile, and maneuverable satellites
within respective constellations relative to the obtained VTWs by each constellation of 100 historical tropical cyclones.
Within the comparative analysis put forth by Ref. [33], the best case of constellation reconfigurability outperforms the
nadir-directional and agile satellite constellations by 450.92 % and 48.52 % on average, respectively, thus depicting
promising results regarding the performance of constellation reconfigurability. Each application of constellation
reconfigurability has demonstrated beneficial results through performance levels significantly greater than those obtained
by fixed constellation configurations. Such research highlights the value that constellation reconfigurability provides
to constellation systems; however, each implementation focuses on either maximum observations (maximum VTW),
minimum revisit times, or a combination of both, hence neglecting data capacity, battery usage, and data downlink, all
of which are key components of the EOSSP.
This paper implements constellation reconfigurability to the EOSSP to develop the Reconfigurable Earth Observation
Satellite Scheduling Problem (REOSSP). Constellation reconfigurability is enabled through recent developments in the
Multistage Constellation Reconfiguration Problem (MCRP) [26вҖ“28] and is motivated through results indicating the
performance level of constellation reconfigurability [32, 33]. The novel REOSSP formulation is developed through
the use of mixed-integer linear programming (MILP), enabling the acquisition of provably optimal solutions and the
use of commercial optimization problem-solving software. Additionally, the presented formulation incorporates the
consideration of onboard data storage, a feature neglected in Refs. [14, 34, 35], as well as onboard battery storage, a
feature neglected in Ref. [15], whereas Refs. [8, 10, 12] neglect both features. Inspiration for the inclusion of such
rigorous considerations originates from Refs. [6, 7, 36] that consider these crucial aspects of the EOSSP.
4


--- Page 5 ---
Furthermore, this paper seeks to improve the computation runtime of the REOSSP through the use of a rolling
horizon procedure (RHP). The RHP approach iteratively optimizes decisions over a given time horizon [37] by splitting
a large problem into multiple subproblems containing a smaller portion of the full problem. As such, each subproblem
has a reduced solution space and thus a reduced level of complexity. This paper presents the computational efficiency
enabled by solving the REOSSP through the use of RHP.
In parallel, a novel EOSSP formulation, similarly considering onboard data and battery storage over time, is
developed as a baseline nonreconfigurable equivalent to the REOSSP to benchmark the performance of the REOSSP
through experimentation. Such experiments include a set of random instances with randomized parameters and a case
study applied to Hurricane Sandy. Through these experiments and the results obtained, the conclusion of Ref. [38] that
constellation reconfigurability greatly outperforms a standard nadir-directional constellation without reconfigurability is
reinforced. Therefore, this paper additionally serves as further proof of concept for the implementation of constellation
reconfigurability to the EOSSP at a higher level of fidelity and operational complexity than previous research.
An illustrative example of the capabilities of the REOSSP is shown in Fig. 1, including a representation of all
tasks and operational constraints. Tasks, such as orbital maneuvering, target observation, data downlink to a ground
station, and solar charging, are shown through arrows in the direction of the task, while the state of variables relating to
operational constraints, such as the battery, data, and fuel capacities, are shown as a level that represents the current
capacity. The initial state of the constellation is at рқ‘Ў0, where both satellites have no visibility of a given target. As a
result of no present visibility, both satellites perform orbital maneuvers to form a more optimal configuration, a process
that drains both the onboard battery and fuel. The reconfigured state of the constellation is at рқ‘Ў1, which has now gained
visibility of the target, thus observing the target and contributing to the onboard data storage while further draining the
onboard battery. At рқ‘Ў2, an observation of the target has been completed and may not be required to be performed again,
leading both satellites to replenish the onboard battery storage through solar charging. Additionally, one satellite in this
portion will not have visibility of the ground station, and as such, this satellite performs an additional orbital maneuver
to gain said visibility. Finally, рқ‘Ў3 shows the final state of the constellation with both satellites downlinking the observed
data to the ground station, transmitting the data currently stored at the cost of more battery power. Furthermore, this
portion shows the additional drain on the fuel of the satellite that performed an additional orbital maneuver.
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Sections II and III introduce the MILP formulations of the EOSSP
and REOSSP, respectively. Then, Sec. IV introduces an algorithmic solution method to the REOSSP using RHP, a
novel formulation referred to as the REOSSP-RHP. Next, Sec. V conducts a thorough comparative analysis between the
EOSSP, REOSSP, and REOSSP-RHP, including instances with randomized parameters and a real-world case study
using historical data from Hurricane Sandy. Finally, Sec. VI provides a review of overall results and contributes several
possible directions for future research.
5


--- Page 6 ---
Fig. 1
A demonstration of the capabilities enabled in the REOSSP.
II. Earth Observation Satellite Scheduling ProblemвҖ”Baseline
To incorporate constellation reconfigurability into the EOSSP, a baseline formulation is developed to be expanded
for reconfiguration, which also proves useful as a comparison between a reconfigurable and a nonreconfigurable solution.
This formulation is henceforth denoted as the EOSSP. The formulation of the EOSSP in this paper is an optimization
problem making use of MILP to schedule tasks of target observation, data downlink to ground stations, and solar panel
charging. Each task is constrained by physical capabilities, data storage availability, and/or battery power availability.
The overall finite schedule duration is designated as рқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ, which is discretized by a time step size О”рқ‘Ў> 0 to result in
the finite number of discrete time steps рқ‘Ү= рқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ/О”рқ‘Ў. The set of time steps, defined as T = {1, 2, . . . ,рқ‘Ү}, contains all
time steps at which a task can be performed. There also exists a set of satellites K = {1, 2, . . . , рқҗҫ} containing рқҗҫtotal
satellites and the associated classical orbital elements, a set of targets for observation and associated positions defined
as P = {1, 2, . . . , рқ‘ғ} where рқ‘ғis the total number of targets, and a set of ground stations for the downlink of data and
associated positions defined as G = {1, 2, . . . , рқҗә} where рқҗәis the total number of ground stations.
The EOSSP also utilizes various parameters within the problem constraints. The visibility of target рқ‘қвҲҲP by
satellite рқ‘ҳвҲҲK at time step рқ‘ЎвҲҲT is contained in рқ‘үрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қas a binary value dependent on if рқ‘қis visible (one) or not (zero).
Similarly, the visibility of ground station рқ‘”вҲҲG by satellite рқ‘ҳat time step рқ‘Ўis contained in рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”and the visibility of the
6


--- Page 7 ---
sun by satellite рқ‘ҳat time step рқ‘Ўis contained in рқҗ»рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, both of which are binary values.
Finally, the data and battery storage levels are tracked for each satellite рқ‘ҳfrom each time step рқ‘Ўto the next рқ‘Ў+ 1. The
current data storage level is determined by tracking the data gained through target observations and the data transmitted
through downlink to any ground station. Additionally, the data storage level of each satellite рқ‘ҳis restricted to not deplete
below a minimum threshold рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min and not accumulate more than the data storage capacity of the satellite, defined as
рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max. Similarly, the current battery storage level is determined by tracking the energy drained by observation, data
downlink, and standard operational tasks such as tracking telemetry and time, and the energy replenished by charging
through sunlight exposure. The battery storage level of each satellite рқ‘ҳis also restricted to not deplete below a minimum
threshold рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
min and not accumulate more than the battery capacity, defined as рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max.
A. Decision Variables and Indicator Variables
The decision variables of the EOSSP are the tasks being scheduled throughout the duration of a given schedule,
with additional indicator variables that track the data storage and battery levels over time. Each decision variable task is
defined as binary such that a value of one corresponds to the task being performed and a value of zero corresponds to the
task not being performed, while the indicator variables are defined as real numbers with upper and lower bounds defined
as the maximum and minimum storage capacities, respectively. Each of these variables is applied to each satellite рқ‘ҳand
time step рқ‘Ўin the overall schedule horizon, while the tasks additionally apply to their associated objective.
The decision variables relative to satellite рқ‘ҳat time step рқ‘Ўinclude the observation of target рқ‘қ, рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қin constraints (1a);
the downlink of data to ground station рқ‘”, рқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”in constraints (1b); and solar charging, в„Һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўin constraints (1c). Similarly,
the indicator variables relative to satellite рқ‘ҳat time step рқ‘Ўinclude: the current data storage level, рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўin constraints (1d),
restricted between a minimum of рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min and a maximum of рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max; and the current battery storage level, рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўin
constraints (1e), restricted between a minimum of рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
min and a maximum of рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max.
рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвҲҲ{0, 1},

B. Objective Function
The objective function of the EOSSP maximizes the number of observations and subsequent downlink occurrences
of data to ground stations, more heavily prioritizing the downlink of data through an arbitrary weight, рқҗ¶> 1. The
objective function value of the EOSSP is denoted as рқ‘§рқҗёobtained via

figure of merit for the EOSSP corresponds to the amount of data downlinked to ground stations, obtained via

where рқҗ·comm вүҘ0 is the amount of data transmitted during a single time step. The objective function value and the
figure of merit are both used to compare the performance of the EOSSP to the REOSSP and REOSSP-RHP.
C. Constraints
The EOSSP contains constraints, including the application of target visibility, ground station visibility, sun visibility,
maximum (and minimum) data storage capacity, and maximum (and minimum) battery storage capacity.
1. Time Window Constraints
Each task is restricted to only be performed when visibility of the task is available to each satellite individually;
these constraints define the VTW for each task utilizing the associated parameter such as рқ‘үрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қfor targets observation,
рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”for ground station downlink, and рқҗ»рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўfor solar panel charging, respectively. Additionally, power-related tasks such
as target observation, data downlink, and solar charging are restricted to occurring exclusively from one another.
рқ‘үрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвүҘрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘қвҲҲP, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(4a)
рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”вүҘрқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘”вҲҲG, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(4b)
рқҗ»рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ЎвүҘв„Һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(4c)
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP

рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”+ в„Һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ЎвүӨ1,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(4d)
8


--- Page 9 ---
Constraints (4aвҖ“4c) restrict the observation of targets, downlink of data to ground stations, and solar charging to the
associated VTW, respectively. These constraints operate under the assumption that only one satellite is required to
perform each task such that each satellite operates independently. Additionally, constraints (4d) restrict observations,
data downlink, and solar charging only to be performed in exclusion to one another, ensuring that each satellite must
choose which task to perform if the VTW of each task overlaps. Such a consideration is in place for battery routing
consideration to not overload the simultaneous power exchange within an onboard battery, similar to the task overlap
exclusion employed in Ref. [39]. Additionally, these constraints ensure that only one target may be viewed at each time
step or that only one ground station may be utilized for data downlink for the same reason.
A demonstration of the visibility conditions in constraints (4aвҖ“4c) as well as the overlap exclusion conditions in
constraints (4d) is shown in Fig. 2. Within the figure, illustrative binary visibility parameters рқ‘үрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, and рқҗ»рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўare
shown through blue, red, and orange boxes, respectively, indicating time along a horizon with visibility of the associated
goal, and the binary decision variables рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, рқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, and в„Һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўare shown similarly. The figure depicts some key occurrences
resulting from the above constraints, labeled as time steps рқ‘Ў1 through рқ‘Ў5. First, time step рқ‘Ў1 depicts the visibility of a
ground station with no downlink occurrence as a result of no prior observations contributing data to be downlinked.
Next time step рқ‘Ў2 depicts the visibility of the sun along with the subsequent solar charging. Then, time step рқ‘Ў3 shows
the visibility and subsequent observation of a target, and time step рқ‘Ў4 illustrates the visibility of a ground station that
allows the downlink of the previously observed data. Finally, time step рқ‘Ў5 depicts the enforcement of constraints (4d),
restricting only one task to occur at each time step, despite all three tasks having visibility of the associated goal at
various times. The enforcement of constraints (4d) additionally applies to the simultaneous visibility of different targets
and ground stations, though this is not shown in the figure directly.

2. Data Tracking and Storage Constraints
The second set of constraints tracks the usage of each satelliteвҖҷs onboard data storage, determining when data is
gained or downlinked and restricting the data so that it does not exceed storage limits.
рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+1 = рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP

рқ‘”вҲҲG

(5a)

рқҗ·obsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвүӨрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(5b)
рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ЎвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ·commрқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”вүҘрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(5c)
Constraints (5a) track the data storage of each satellite at each time step, ensuring that the tasks performed at time
step рқ‘Ўcontribute to the data storage level at time step рқ‘Ў+ 1, where рқҗ·obs вүҘ0 is the amount of data acquired through one
observation within one time step. Furthermore, рқҗ·obs is a constant value of given units, while рқҗ·comm is obtained by
multiplying a data transmission rate in units/time by О”рқ‘Ўto obtain a discrete constant value. It is also assumed that each
satellite begins with the minimum data storage value such that рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ
1 = рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, allowing the first data observed to
contribute at time рқ‘Ў= 1 for the data storage level of рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ
2. Constraints (5b) ensure that the data stored within each satellite
through observation does not exceed the maximum capacity set by рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max. Similarly, constraints (5c) ensure that the data
transmitted to a ground station does not exceed the actual amount of data contained by the satellite.
A demonstration of the control and contribution of the data storage for each satellite is shown in Fig. 3a, with similar
key occurrences denoted by time steps рқ‘Ў1 through рқ‘Ў5. First, time steps рқ‘Ў1 and рқ‘Ў2 depict the contribution of observations
and the drain of downlink to the onboard data storage, respectively, provided through constraints (5a). Then, time steps
рқ‘Ў3 and рқ‘Ў4 depict the halt of observations such that the onboard data level does not exceed the maximum capacity set by
рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max, provided through constraints (5b). Finally, time step рқ‘Ў5 depicts the halt of downlink once the data level is fully
drained, ensuring the current data level remains non-negative, provided through constraints (5c).
3. Battery Tracking and Storage Constraints
The third and final set of constraints tracks the usage of the onboard battery power of each satellite, similarly to that
of the data tracking constraints, determining when the battery is charged or depleted, as well as restricting the power to
10


--- Page 11 ---
not overflow onboard battery limits and not deplete below the minimum capacity.
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+1 = рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+ рқҗөchargeв„Һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ЎвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқҗөobsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗөcommрқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”вҲ’рқҗөtime,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT \ {рқ‘Ү}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(6a)
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+ рқҗөchargeв„Һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ЎвүӨрқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(6b)
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ЎвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқҗөobsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗөcommрқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”вҲ’рқҗөtime вүҘрқҗөрқ‘ҳ
min,
вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(6c)
Constraints (6a) track the battery level of each satellite at each time step, where рқҗөcharge вүҘ0 is the amount of power
able to be charged, рқҗөobs вүҘ0 is the amount of power required for observation to occur, рқҗөcomm вүҘ0 is the amount of power
required for a data downlink transmission, and рқҗөtime вүҘ0 is the power required for normal operational tasks such as
telemetry and timekeeping. Each power value accounts for one time step and is found by multiplying a charge rate in
units/time by О”рқ‘Ўto obtain a power value in the given units. It is similarly assumed that each satellite begins with a
full battery charge such that рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
1 = рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, allowing the first power draw occurring at рқ‘Ў= 1 to contribute to the
battery storage level of рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳ
2. Constraints (6b) ensure that the power contained within each satellite through charging does
not exceed the maximum capacity of the battery set by рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max. Similarly, constraints (6c) ensure that the power required
by other task performance does not drain the battery below the minimum capacity set by рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
min.
Figure 3b visualizes the battery consumption similarly to Fig. 3a, with key occurrences denoted by time steps рқ‘Ў1
through рқ‘Ў4. First, time step рқ‘Ў1 demonstrates the constant battery drain caused by normal operational tasks, рқҗөtime, and time
step рқ‘Ў2 demonstrates the combined battery drain of the constant drain and observation. Then, time step рқ‘Ў3 demonstrates
the battery charging as a result of charging operations while within sun visibility. Finally, time step рқ‘Ў4 reiterates the
task overlap exclusion from constraints (4d). Such consumption of battery power additionally applies to data downlink,
though it is not shown directly in the figure.
рқҗ·max
рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўрқ‘қ
рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘һрқ‘Ўрқ‘”
рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘‘рқ‘Ў
рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў1
рқ‘Ў2
рқ‘Ў3
рқ‘Ў4
рқ‘Ў5
рқҗ·min
рқ‘ҳ

(a) Visualization of the data storage level of satellite рқ‘ҳ

в„Һрқ‘Ў
рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘Ў

рқҗөmax

Fig. 3
Data and battery storage level visualizations of satellite рқ‘ҳ.

--- Page 12 ---
D. Full Formulation
Given the objective function, constraints, and decision/indicator variables, the full EOSSP is defined as follows:
max
рқ‘§рқҗё
s.t.
Constraints (1)вҖ“(2), (4)вҖ“(6)
(EOSSP)
The objective function balances the number of occurrences of data downlink to any ground stations рқ‘”вҲҲG with the
less heavily weighted number of occurrences of the observation of any targets рқ‘қвҲҲP, while the constraints restrict the
problem to the observation of data before data downlink, the assurance of available data storage, and the assurance of
available power for task completion. Additionally, the EOSSP provides the schedule of charging, observation, and data
downlink performed by a constellation of satellites over a given time horizon.
III. Reconfigurable Earth Observation Satellite Scheduling Problem
The REOSSP formulation, henceforth referred to as the REOSSP, is an extension of the EOSSP that incorporates
satellite maneuvering tasks in addition to the tasks present in the EOSSP, thus allowing a new degree of freedom. The
constellation reconfiguration process is derived from the MCRP, obtained from Refs. [26вҖ“28]. All sets and parameters
remain as defined in Sec. II unless otherwise specified.
Initial changes are made to T regarding stages for reconfiguration, as well as additional orbital slots being provided
to each satellite рқ‘ҳвҲҲK. Firstly, within T there exists a set of equally spaced stages at which each satellite in the
constellation can perform orbital maneuvers, defined as S = {0, 1, 2, . . . , рқ‘Ҷ}, where рқ‘Ҷis the number of equally spaced
stages and stage рқ‘ = 0 is the stage wherein the initial configuration of the constellation is present. As a result of each
stage being equally spaced in T, there exists a set of time steps within each stage defined as T рқ‘ = {1, 2, . . . ,рқ‘Үрқ‘ }
where рқ‘Үрқ‘ = рқ‘Ү/рқ‘Ҷis an integer defining the number of time steps per stage. Secondly, the orbital slots are defined as
J рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ= {1, 2, . . . , рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ}, where рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳis the total number of available slots for satellite рқ‘ҳвҲҲK and stage рқ‘ вҲҲS.
As a result of the REOSSP dividing the time horizon into stages, some parameters are modified slightly, and
additional parameters are included for the cost of orbital maneuvers. Most importantly, the binary visibility parameters
for targets, ground stations, and the sun are extended to include two additional dimensions representing the current stage
and occupied orbital slot; each is now defined as рқ‘үрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘қ, рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘”, and рқҗ»рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—, respectively. Additionally, the cost of transfer
between two orbital slots of a given satellite and a given stage is рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, defined as the transfer between slot рқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳin
the previous stage configuration (рқ‘ вҲ’1) to slot рқ‘—вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳin the current stage (рқ‘ ) relative to satellite рқ‘ҳand stage рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}.
Furthermore, the maximum transfer cost for the entire schedule is allotted to each satellite as рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK.
12


--- Page 13 ---
A. Decision Variables
The decision variables and indicator variables of the REOSSP are the same tasks and indicators included in the
EOSSP, with additional dimensionality regarding reconfiguration stages and an additional task to control the maneuvers
of each satellite. The new decision variable, рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—in constraints (7a), defines the sequence of maneuvers of satellite
рқ‘ҳfrom an orbital slot рқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳin the previous stage to an orbital slot рқ‘—вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳin the current stage for all stages
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0} such that the singleton set initial condition orbital slot option, J 0рқ‘ҳ, is transferred from stage рқ‘ = 1. All other
decision and indicator variables expand the dimensionality of the respective EOSSP variables through an additional
index рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0} relative to time step рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ and are represented in constraints (7bвҖ“7e):
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—вҲҲ{0, 1},
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘ҳвҲҲK, рқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳ, рқ‘—вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
(7a)
рқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвҲҲ{0, 1},
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , рқ‘қвҲҲP, рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(7b)
рқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”вҲҲ{0, 1},
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , рқ‘”вҲҲG, рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(7c)
в„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
вҲҲ{0, 1},
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(7d)
рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
вҲҲ[рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min, рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max],
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(7e)
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
вҲҲ[рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
min, рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max],
рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(7f)
B. Objective Function
The objective function of the REOSSP mirrors the EOSSP, maximizing the number of observations and subsequent
downlink occurrences of data to ground stations with the same weighting рқҗ¶to prioritize the downlink of data. The
objective function value of the REOSSP is denoted as рқ‘§рқ‘…obtained via
рқ‘§рқ‘…=
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ вҲҲS\{0}
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ЎвҲҲTрқ‘ 
В©Вӯ
В«
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ¶рқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ
ВӘВ®
В¬
(8)
where рқ‘§рқ‘…additionally considers all рқ‘Ҷstages and all рқ‘Үрқ‘ time steps per stage. Similarly to the EOSSP, the figure of merit
for the REOSSP corresponds to the amount of data downlinked to ground stations, obtained via
рқ‘Қрқ‘…=
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ вҲҲS\{0}
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ЎвҲҲTрқ‘ 
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ·commрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”
(9)
The objective function value and the figure of merit are used to compare the performance of the REOSSP to the EOSSP
and REOSSP-RHP. To distinguish the resultant objective function values and figure of merit values, the subscript рқ‘…is
used in place of the subscript рқҗё.
13


--- Page 14 ---
C. Constraints
The REOSSP contains similar constraints to the EOSSP but with extended dimensionality to incorporate stages
sequentially within the time horizon and reflect the effects of the reconfiguration process through new satellite positioning
and resources required to perform such maneuvers.
1. Orbital Maneuver Path Continuity Constraints
The first set of constraints restricts the path of constellation reconfigurability to feasible maneuvering sequences
concerning a given budget for propellant.
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘—вҲҲJ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—= 1,
вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘–вҲҲJ 0рқ‘ҳ
(10a)
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘—вҲҲJрқ‘ +1,рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ +1,рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—
вҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘—вҖІвҲҲJрқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘—вҖІрқ‘–= 0,
вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0, рқ‘Ҷ}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
(10b)
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ вҲҲS\{0}
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘–вҲҲJрқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳ
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘—вҲҲJрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—вүӨрқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max,
вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(10c)
Constraints (10a) ensure that only one orbital slot in the first stage (J 1рқ‘ҳ) can be selected for transfer from the initial
condition (J 0рқ‘ҳ), ensuring that one satellite cannot occupy multiple positions at one time. Constraints (10b) similarly
ensure that only one orbital slot in subsequent stages is selected for transfer but also extend the condition to state that the
satellite can only transfer from the current slot (рқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ) if it arrived there previously. Both constraints ensure that
satellites do not conduct transfers from positions in which they may not be located. Finally, constraints (10c) restrict the
allowable transfer between slots to within the maximum propellant budget of the satellite.
A demonstration of feasible path continuity and maneuvering is shown in Fig. 4, depicting the possible paths taken
by any number of satellites for any number of orbital slots or stages, where the highlighted paths represent рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—= 1.
The figure notes that the initial condition and first orbital maneuvers occur outside of the time horizon such that the
constellation configuration at stage рқ‘ = 1 is formed at time рқ‘Ў= 1 and subsequent stages occur at the corresponding
рқ‘Ў= 1 + (рқ‘ вҲ’1)рқ‘Үрқ‘ time. Additionally, the figure demonstrates the option to remain within the current orbital slot, such as
satellite рқҗҫin stage two, resulting in a cost рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—of zero.
2. Time Window Constraints
The second set of constraints restricts task performance to the available VTWs, additionally enforcing the task
overlap exclusion of each task. These constraints mirror constraints (4) while additionally accounting for the orbital
maneuver path and subsequent satellite orbital slot positions within each reconfiguration stage, incorporated using рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—

рқ‘Ўрқ‘”,

вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(11d)
3. Data Tracking and Storage Constraints
The third set of constraints tracks the usage of the onboard data storage level of each satellite, mirroring constraints (5),
while additionally splitting constraints (5a) between constraints (12a) and (12b). Constraints (12a) track the data storage
level of each satellite within the time horizon of each stage, while constraints (12b) track the data storage level at the
stage gap, where the end of one stage meets the beginning of the next, providing the condition that рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ +1 = рқ‘‘рқ‘ +1,рқ‘ҳ
1
.
15


--- Page 16 ---
рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+1 = рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқҗ·obsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ·commрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”,
вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ \ {рқ‘Үрқ‘ }, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(12a)
рқ‘‘рқ‘ +1,рқ‘ҳ
1
= рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ +
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқҗ·obsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘қвҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ·commрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘”,
вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0, рқ‘Ҷ}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(12b)
рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқҗ·obsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қвүӨрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max,
вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK

min,
вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(12d)
4. Battery Tracking Constraints
The fourth set of constraints only tracks the onboard battery capacity, mirroring constraints (6a). Similarly to
constraints (12a) and (12b), constraints (13a) and (13b) track the battery storage level of each satellite within the time
horizon of each stage and at the stage gap, respectively, where рқҗөrecon вүҘ0 is the power cost of orbital maneuvers as a
conservative estimate for thruster pointing. Separately, constraints (13c) account for the first orbital maneuvers, defined
as a reduction from a full onboard battery of рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max.
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў+1 = рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
+ рқҗөchargeв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
вҲ’
вҲ‘пёҒ

вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(13c)
5. Battery Storage Constraints
The final set of constraints ensures the onboard battery level remains within established bounds, where constraints (14a)
and (14b) are direct parallels to constraints (6b) and (6c), respectively. Additionally, constraints (14c) enforce the same
condition as constraints (14b) at the stage gaps, and constraints (14d) account for the first orbital maneuvers.
16


--- Page 17 ---
рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
+ рқҗөchargeв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
вүӨрқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max,
вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK

(14d)
D. Full Formulation
Given the new objective function, constraints, and decision variables, with the addition of orbital maneuvering as a
task and the reconfiguration stages, the full REOSSP is denoted as follows:
max
рқ‘§рқ‘…
s.t.
Constraints (7)вҖ“(8), (10)вҖ“(14)
(REOSSP)
The objective function again balances the number of occurrences of data downlink to any ground stations рқ‘”вҲҲG
with the less heavily weighted number of occurrences of the observation of any targets рқ‘қвҲҲP. Simultaneously, the
constraints restrict the problem to the observation of data prior to data downlink, the assurance of available data
storage, the assurance of available battery power, and feasible constellation reconfigurability with consideration of a
maximum fuel budget. Additionally, the REOSSP provides the schedule of charging, observation, data downlink, and
orbital maneuvers, as well as the path of orbital maneuvers, of a constellation of satellites over a given time horizon.
Appendix A contains a mathematical proof validating the identical nature of the EOSSP and REOSSP in the event that
рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max = 0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, wherein no satellite may perform orbital maneuvers.
IV. Rolling Horizon Procedure
The RHP solution method is an iterative algorithmic approach to the REOSSP that divides the problem into
sequential subproblems, each incorporating future lookahead stages and updating the problem state as the subproblems
are solved at reconfiguration stages. This solution method is introduced to address the computational intractability
that may occur in large instances of the REOSSP and will be referred to as the REOSSP-RHP for brevity. The
17


--- Page 18 ---
REOSSP-RHP analyzes the impact of decisions on future lookahead stages to make a more informed decision in the
current stage with a much smaller problem scale.
The REOSSP-RHP heavily mirrors the REOSSP in all aspects, with a few key differences through the expansion
of time parameters to separate the problem into piecewise subproblems along the time horizon. As such, only the
differences between the REOSSP-RHP and REOSSP will be explicitly stated. Each subproblem, denoted RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ),
consisted of a control stage рқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0} in which the solution is isolated and рқҗҝlookahead stages which aid in the
decision-making process. Therefore, the REOSSP-RHP reformats an рқ‘Ҷ-stage REOSSP to рқ‘ҶвҲ’рқҗҝtotal subproblems,
and the cumulative solution of the REOSSP-RHP is composed of the solutions to each subproblem. Within each
subproblem, the parameter в„“вҲҲL = {рқ‘ , . . . , рқ‘ + рқҗҝ} is used to denote the lookahead stage within a subproblem, replacing
the рқ‘ used in the REOSSP (e.g., рқ‘үв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘қand рқ‘җв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—).
Certain parameters that are relevant throughout the entire schedule duration must be updated within each subproblem
of the REOSSP-RHP. Firstly, the maximum transfer cost budget of each subproblem depends on the orbital maneuvers
performed within previous subproblems; therefore, the maximum transfer cost budget of each subproblem is given
as рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
max, which is obtained via Eq. (15a), where Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—is the optimal orbital maneuver decision variable from previous
subproblems. Secondly, the initial satellite orbital slots of each control stage depend on the orbital maneuvers performed
within previous subproblems; therefore, the set ЛңJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳis defined according to Eq. (15b). Finally, the initial data storage
and battery storage values depend upon the tasks performed within previous subproblems, wherein these values will be
assigned as рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
1 and рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
1 obtained via Eqs. (15c) and (15d), respectively. In Eqs. (15c) and (15d), the decision variables
Лңрқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, and Лңв„Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
correspond to the optimal decision variables of target observation, ground station downlink, and solar
charging within previous subproblems, respectively. With respect to the first stage рқ‘ = 1, all values are assumed to be
the same as in the REOSSP such that рқ‘җ1рқ‘ҳ
max = рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, рқ‘‘1рқ‘ҳ
1 = рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, and рқ‘Ҹ1рқ‘ҳ
1 = рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK.
рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
max = рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max вҲ’
рқ‘ вҲ’1
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ӣ=1

рқ‘җрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—
(15a)

(15b)

A. Decision Variables and Indicator Variables
Each subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) contains the same decision and indicator variables as the REOSSP, with additional
consideration that J в„“вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳis only a singleton set when рқ‘ = 1.

(16a)

(16f)
B. Objective Function
As with the REOSSP, the objective function of each subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) maximizes the number of observations
and subsequent downlink occurrences of data to ground stations with weighting рқҗ¶to prioritize the downlink of data.
The objective function value of each subproblem is denoted as рқ‘§рқ‘ 
RHP obtained via
рқ‘§рқ‘ 
RHP =
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
вҲ‘пёҒ
в„“вҲҲL
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ЎвҲҲTв„“
В©Вӯ
В«
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ¶рқ‘һв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқ‘Ұв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ
ВӘВ®
В¬
(17)
where рқ‘§рқ‘ 
RHP is the objective function value of subproblem рқ‘ . Additionally, a figure of merit рқ‘ҚRHP is defined for the
REOSSP-RHP at the end of this section relative to the optimal solution to each subproblem. The subscript RHP is used
to distinguish between the objective function values and figures of merit for the EOSSP and REOSSP.
C. Constraints
Each subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) expands the functionality of the constraints of the REOSSP according to the updating
equations Eq. (15), which update key parameters between subproblems.
19


--- Page 20 ---
1. Orbital Maneuver Path Continuity Constraints
The first set of constraints mirror constraints (10), while constraints (18a) and (18c) depend on the updated parameters
ЛңJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳand рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
max, respectively.

2. Time Window Constraints
The second set of constraints directly mirror constraints (11) relating to VTWs and task performance opportunities.

рқ‘Ўрқ‘”,
вҲҖв„“вҲҲL, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT в„“, вҲҖрқ‘”вҲҲG, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(19b)
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘–вҲҲJв„“вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳ
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘—вҲҲJв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқҗ»в„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—вүҘв„Һв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў,
вҲҖв„“вҲҲL, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT в„“, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(19c)
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘қвҲҲP
рқ‘Ұв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ+
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқ‘һв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”+ в„Һв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў
вүӨ1,
вҲҖв„“вҲҲL, вҲҖрқ‘ЎвҲҲT в„“, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
(19d)
3. Data Tracking and Storage Constraints
The data tracking and storage constraints in each subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) closely mirror constraints (12), with further
breakdown pertaining to the consideration of the updated parameter рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
1 from Eq. (15c). As such, constraints (20a)
account for the updated parameter, constraints (20b) account for the rest of the рқ‘ stage, constraints (20c) account for times
within each other stage, and constraints (20d) account for the stage gaps. Constraints (20e) and (20f) are unchanged
apart from the consideration of в„“.
20

breakdown relative to the updated parameter рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
1 from Eq. (15d). Constraints (21a) account for the updated parameter,
constraints (21b) account for the rest of the рқ‘ stage, constraints (21c) account for times within all other stages, and
constraints (21d) account for the stage gaps.

5. Battery Storage Constraints
Finally, the battery storage constraints of each subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) directly mirror constraints (14) for the feasible
restriction of battery capacities.

(22d)
D. Full Formulation
With the definition of the objective function, constraints, and decision variables, subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) is given as
max
рқ‘§рқ‘ 
RHP
s.t.
Constraints (16)вҖ“(22)
(RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ))
Within each subproblem, the initial conditions of the control stage рқ‘ are updated using Eqs. (15). The subproblem
RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) is then solved iteratively for all control stages рқ‘ вҲҲ{1, 2, . . . , рқ‘ҶвҲ’рқҗҝ} through the use of the full REOSSP-RHP
algorithm, defined in Algorithm 1, where the objective of each subproblem RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) is to maximize the number of
downlink occurrences to any ground station and the number of target observations, with a heavier weighting on the
number of downlink occurrences. The constraints restrict the data and battery storage values of each satellite, as well as
limit the propellant budget for orbital maneuvers. Upon obtainment of the solution of each subproblem, the optimal
decision variables for the first stage of each subproblem are appended linearly within the time horizon to obtain the set
of optimal decision variables throughout the entire time horizon, given as ( Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, Лңрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, Лңв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў) in Algorithm 1.
The REOSSP-RHP provides the schedule of each satellite, including the charging, observing, downlinking, and orbital
maneuvering sequence to provide a complete overview of the tasks performed by each satellite. The REOSSP-RHP
includes a figure of merit found using the optimal solution, corresponding to the amount of data downlinked to ground
stations by the optimal decision variable Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”and obtained via
22


--- Page 23 ---
рқ‘ҚRHP =
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ҳвҲҲK
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ вҲҲS\{0}
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘ЎвҲҲTрқ‘ 
вҲ‘пёҒ
рқ‘”вҲҲG
рқҗ·comm Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”
(23)
Algorithm 1: REOSSP-RHP
1 for рқ‘ = 1, . . . , рқ‘ҶвҲ’рқҗҝвҲ’1 do
2
if рқ‘ вүҘ2 then
3
Update рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
max, ЛңJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳ, рқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
1 , and рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
1 Using Eq. (15)
4
Solve RHP(рқ‘ , рқҗҝ) and store: рқ‘§рқ‘ 
RHP and ( Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, Лңрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, Лңв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў)
5 рқ‘ вҶҗрқ‘ҶвҲ’рқҗҝ
6 Solve RHP(рқ‘ҶвҲ’рқҗҝ, рқҗҝ) and store: {рқ‘§1
RHP, . . . , рқ‘§рқ‘Ҷ
RHP} and
( Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, Лңрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, Лңв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў) вү”{( Лңрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, Лңрқ‘Ұ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, Лңв„Һ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘‘1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘Ҹ1рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў), . . . , ( Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, Лңрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, Лңв„Һрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘‘рқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў)}
7 return ( Лңрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—, Лңрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қ, Лңрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”, Лңв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў, Лңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ў)
V. Computational Experiments
The REOSSP, solved using a commercial software (therefore theoretically returning an exact optimal solution if
given adequate computational resources and runtime) and referred to as the REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP are
benchmarked against the EOSSP and compared to one another through a wide variety of schedule characteristics to
evaluate the performance of each formulation relative to a baseline nonreconfigurable scheduling solution. Benchmarking
experiments are conducted in two ways: including random instances of randomly varied schedule characteristics
and a case study involving Hurricane Sandy, a highly dynamic natural disaster. The objective function values, рқ‘§рқҗё,
рқ‘§рқ‘…, and рқ‘§RHP for the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP, respectively, compare the performance of each
scheduling problem. Additionally, the figures of merit, VTW of targets and ground stations, amount of data observed
but not downlinked, amount of battery required to perform operations, and the orbital maneuver cost consumed by the
satellites in the resulting schedules of the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP are presented for analysis. Finally, the
resultant schedule is used to gain insight into the order of task performance, especially concerning the case study. All
computational experiments assume deterministic problems, where characteristics of a schedule are known a priori.
All random instances and case studies are computed on a platform equipped with an Intel Core i9-12900 2.4 GHz
(base frequency) CPU processor (16 cores and 24 logical processors) and 64 GB of RAM. While these specifications are
not indicative of the capabilities of a satellite for onboard scheduling, they are relevant to demonstrate the computational
efficiency of the formulations tested. Additionally, the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP are programmed
in MATLAB [40] with the use of YALMIP [41] and are solved using the commercial software package Gurobi Optimizer
(version 12.0.0) with default settings apart from an assigned runtime.
23


--- Page 24 ---
A. Random Instances
A set of 24 random instances with random schedule characteristics demonstrates the capability of each scheduling
problem in response to varied parameters. Each instance draws combinations of given parameters, creating variation in
the number of stages, satellites, and orbital slot options available. The sets for such combinations are рқ‘ҶвҲҲ{8, 9, 12},
рқҗҫвҲҲ{5, 6}, and рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳвҲҲ{20, 40, 60, 80}, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, respectively. The Gurobi runtime limit for the EOSSP,
REOSSP-Exact, and each subproblem of the REOSSP-RHP is set to 60 min (3600 s).
1. Design of Random Instances
Each of the 24 random instances is unique to ensure that no instances have identical parameters, providing variation
in ground station position, satellite orbits, and target position such that each scheduling problem is compared under a
wide spectrum of conditions, thus not limiting the evaluation to a possibly biased set of conditions and providing a
dynamic target through varied available rewards. All рқҗҫsatellites are assigned inclined circular orbits with random
orbital elements within specified ranges, such as an altitude between 600 and 1200 km, inclination between 40 and
80 deg, right ascension of ascending node (RAAN) between 0 and 360 deg, and argument of latitude between 0 and
360 deg. Additionally, all рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳorbital slots vary only in the argument of latitude (herein referred to as phase) and are
equally spaced between 0 and 360 deg with the inclusion of the initial phase. The number of ground stations is set as
two distinct ground stations (рқҗә= 2) and each is randomly assigned a latitude between 80 deg South and 80 deg North
and a longitude between 180 deg West and 180 deg East, with an extra condition specifying that the ground station must
be on land. The number of targets is set as 12 distinct targets (рқ‘ғ= 12) where each target position varies on the same
latitude and longitude range as the ground stations, with allowance of targets on water. Each target is assigned a binary
masking within the visibility matrix рқ‘үрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қand рқ‘үрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘қsuch that each target only has a visibility value of one on a time
interval of рқ‘Ү/рқ‘ғ, thus allowing exactly one target to be considered as available at each time step.
Certain parameters are fixed in each of the 24 random instances, where all fixed parameters are included in Table 2,
with necessary explanation following. Both рқҗ·obs and рқҗ·comm are converted from values in Mbps to discrete values in
MB corresponding to a single time step. Additionally, рқҗ·obs is the average of the two observation rates from Ref. [42],
and рқҗ·comm is the X-band frequency of the SSTL-300 S1 spacecraft capable of Binary Phase-shift keying modulation
from Ref. [43]. Due to the small difference between рқҗ·obs and рқҗ·comm, not all data observed in a single occurrence
can be downlinked in a single occurrence, thus leaving a small amount of data in storage at the end of the schedule
horizon. Furthermore, рқҗөobs, рқҗөcomm, рқҗөrecon, рқҗөcharge, and рқҗөtime are converted from kW to kJ, similarly to рқҗ·obs and рқҗ·comm.
Additionally, the value of рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max is converted from a 15 A-h Battery using the average between 28 and 33 V as obtained
from Ref. [43], and the value of рқҗөcharge is generated using the specification of 2.44 m2 gallium arsenide solar cells from
Ref. [43] for power generation at 160 to 180 W/m2 [44] in which the average of 170 W is used.
In addition to the parameters listed, key methods are used to generate рқ‘ү, рқ‘Ҡ, рқҗ», and рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—. Visibility matrices such as
24


--- Page 25 ---
Table 2
Fixed random instance parameters
Parameter
Value
Units
Parameter
Value
Units
Schedule start time
January 1st, 2025, 00:00:00, UTC
вҖ”
рқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ
14
days
О”рқ‘Ў
100
s
рқ‘Ү
12 096
вҖ”
рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
min, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
0
GB
рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
128 [43]
GB
рқҗ·obs
102.50 [42]
MB
рқҗ·comm
100 [43]
MB
рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
min, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
0
kJ
рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
1647 [43]
kJ
рқҗөobs
16.26 [42]
kJ
рқҗөcomm
1.20 [43]
kJ
рқҗөrecon
0.50 [43]
kJ
рқҗөcharge
41.48 [43]
kJ
рқҗөtime
2 [43]
kJ
рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK
750
m/s
рқҗ¶
2
вҖ”
рқҗҝ
1
вҖ”
рқ‘үand рқҗәare generated through the use of the Aerospace Toolbox [40] by propagating satellite orbits and orbital slots,
assigning each satellite a conical field of view (FOV) of 45 deg for target observation, a conical communication range of
120 deg for ground station communication, and evaluating the access function. This access function returns a binary
value representing whether a given target object (either a target or ground station) is within the satellite FOV at any
time step over the schedule duration. Separately, the visibility matrix рқҗ»is generated through the same propagation and
the use of the eclipse function. This eclipse function returns a value between zero and one representing sunlight
exposure via the dual cone method, with direct sunlight assigned one, penumbra assigned between zero and one, and
umbra assigned zero [45]. The propagator used is the Simplified General Perturbation 4 (SGP4) model. Finally, the
transfer cost рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—is computed through the use of the circular coplanar phasing problem found in Ref. [46], in which
orbital slot рқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳand рқ‘—вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳare defined as the boundary conditions.
It should be noted that the chosen parameters for the random instances are specially selected to demonstrate the
use and results of the formulated scheduling problems. Parameters such as constellation formation, satellite FOV,
communication range, maximum transfer budget, and others, can be chosen to model desired EO applications.
2. Random Instance Results
Table 3 contains the results of all 24 random instances with associated specified varied parameters and ID numbers.
The results include the optimal objective function values (or sum of all subproblem objective function values for
the REOSSP-RHP), the figures of merit, and the computational runtime for the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and
REOSSP-RHP. The percent improvement of one schedule resultant objective function value over another is designated
by рқӣҫ. Additionally, the results include the total propellant used by the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP in km/s.
Finally, the results include the minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation values of the runtime, the total
propellant used, and рқӣҫvalues, where the reported values of the REOSSP-Exact omit the instances that did not result in
a feasible solution.
25


--- Page 26 ---

a A dash (-) indicates the trigger of the runtime limit of 3600 s without an obtained feasible solution.
b Gurobi error code 10001 (out of memory) occurred, and no feasible solution was obtained.
c Although the runtime limit of 3600 s was reached, a feasible solution was obtained. IDs 18 and 21 include a mixed integer programming gap of 3.65 % and 0.28 %, respectively.
26


--- Page 27 ---
Both the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP outperform the EOSSP in every instance, apart from IDs
3, 5, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15, 19, 22, and 23 in which the REOSSP-Exact triggers the runtime limit of 3600 s without ob-
taining a feasible solution and IDs 4, 16, 20, and 24 in which the REOSSP-Exact triggers Gurobi error code 10001
(out of memory), while the REOSSP-RHP still outperforms the EOSSP in these instances. Additionally, there are no
instances in which the REOSSP-RHP outperforms the REOSSP-Exact, apart from those in which the REOSSP-Exact
does not obtain a feasible solution. This is expected since the REOSSP-Exact has access to the entire schedule horizon
when optimizing, while the REOSSP-RHP only has access to a significantly reduced portion of the schedule. However,
the REOSSP-RHP performs computations significantly faster than the REOSSP-Exact as a result of the significantly
reduced problem scale within each subproblem of the REOSSP-RHP, requiring less computation runtime in all instances
apart from ID 13, where the REOSSP-RHP required approximately an additional 6 s overall. The percent improvement
of the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP over the EOSSP, as well as the runtime of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact,
and REOSSP-RHP, is shown as box charts in Fig. 5a and 5b, respectively. Similar figures depicting the metrics of each
individual instance are located in Appendix B.
REOSSP-RHP
REOSSP-Exact
Random instance, ID
0
50
100
150
Percent improvement, %
(a) Percent of the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP over the
EOSSP
EOSSP
REOSSP-RHP
REOSSP-Exact
Random instance, ID
0
10
20
30
40
50
60
70
Computational runtime, min
(b) Runtime of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-
RHP
Fig. 5
Results of the random instances.
Statistically, the REOSSP-Exact outperforms the EOSSP with an average improvement of 101.80 % with a standard
deviation of 17.73 %, a minimum improvement of 0.00 % (IDs 3вҖ“5, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15, 16, 19, 20, 22вҖ“24), and a maximum
improvement of 136.21 % (ID 18). Similarly, the REOSSP-RHP outperforms the EOSSP with an average improvement
of 78.06 % with a standard deviation of 18.17 %, a minimum improvement of 45.24 % (ID 13), and a maximum
improvement of 116.67 % (ID 22). Furthermore, while the REOSSP-RHP performs worse than the REOSSP-Exact by
an average of 13.40 % with a standard deviation of 4.20 %, the REOSSP-RHP attains significant improvement over the
EOSSP in the event that the REOSSP-Exact does not obtain a feasible solution within the time limit, during which the
REOSSP-RHP improves upon the EOSSP by an average of 80.41 %. Additionally, the REOSSP-RHP performs better
relative to the REOSSP-Exact when the number of stages is lower as a result of the larger рқ‘Үрқ‘ value and thus larger
subproblem size providing more problem information in each subproblem, while the REOSSP-RHP performs worse as
27


--- Page 28 ---
the number of stages increases.
Additionally, the runtime of the REOSSP-Exact is much greater than that of the EOSSP, while the average runtime
of the REOSSP-RHP is slightly more than one-third of the REOSSP-Exact runtime limit of 3600 s. Contrarily, the
runtime of the REOSSP-RHP is greater than the runtime of the REOSSP-Exact in ID 13 and the runtime of the
REOSSP-RHP is greater than the runtime limit of the REOSSP-Exact in ID 8. Statistically, the runtime of the
REOSSP-Exact is 29.43 min on average with a standard deviation of 19.40 min, a minimum of 4.55 min (ID 1), and a
maximum beyond the runtime limit in each instance with no feasible solution obtained as well as IDs 18 and 21, which
obtained a suboptimal feasible solution after triggering the runtime limit. Separately, the runtime of the REOSSP-RHP
is lower at 22.74 min on average with a standard deviation of 19.73 min, a minimum of 1.91 min (ID 1), and a maximum
of 67.61 min (ID 8). Additionally, the minimum runtime instance of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP
is ID 1, wherein the lowest combination of рқҗҫand рқҗҪare present, while the maximum runtime instances of each scheduling
problem occur in the event that the larger values of рқҗҫand рқҗҪare present. As for the maximum runtime instance of
the REOSSP-RHP (ID 8), the highest combination of рқҗҫand рқҗҪare present, while рқ‘Ҷis the lowest possible value, thus
resulting in the largest рқ‘Үрқ‘ value and thus the largest subproblem size. As for those IDs where the REOSSP-Exact does
not obtain a feasible solution, the cause is twofold. The first cause originates in the complexity of the problem geometry,
wherein satellite visibility overlap may be complicated. Separately, the second cause is due to the combinatorial nature
of the problem; the solution space expands with the increase in decision variables, which is heavily influenced by the
number of satellites and orbital slot options.
Finally, the main limitation of constellation reconfigurability is the fuel cost required to perform orbital maneuvers
throughout the schedule horizon, wherein the optimal schedule of the REOSSP-RHP uses more propellant than that of
the REOSSP-Exact. The average propellant used by the REOSSP-Exact is 3.41 km/s with a standard deviation of
0.46 km/s (with the removal of the instances where no feasible solution was found). Meanwhile, the REOSSP-RHP is
more expensive, where the average propellant used is 4.04 km/s with a slightly lower standard deviation of 0.38 km/s.
The REOSSP-RHP is more expensive than the REOSSP-Exact on average as a result of the limited information
presented to each subproblem, potentially leading to slightly less optimal orbital maneuvers taking place.
Overall, the average performance increase over the EOSSP by both the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP shows
that each solution method can greatly outperform the EOSSP despite the limited fuel budget for orbital maneuvers
and additional complexity. Additionally, the value of the REOSSP-RHP is demonstrated through the attainment of a
feasible solution in a reduced amount of time on average, especially in those cases where the REOSSP-Exact did not
obtain a feasible solution. Furthermore, the REOSSP-RHP outperforms the REOSSP-Exact on average on account of
the REOSSP-Exact instances that did not result in a feasible solution.
28


--- Page 29 ---
B. Case Study - Hurricane Sandy
In addition to the random instances, Hurricane Sandy is selected for an in-depth analysis to provide real-world data
to the scheduling problems. Hurricane Sandy occurred in 2012 and struck many islands throughout the Caribbean
Sea as well as the Northeastern coast of the United States, causing 233 deaths [47] and $65 billion in damage [48].
Hurricane Sandy achieved Category Three status on October 25 at 6:00 AM with 115 mph winds at the peak [49].
1. Design of Case Study
For the case study, fixed schedule characteristics are selected rather than random characteristics to demonstrate
realistic utilization of the scheduling problems further. Firstly, Hurricane Sandy is depicted in Fig. 6a from the initial to
the final occurrence of tropical storm status or higher (defined as containing winds between 39 and 73 mph [50]). The
historical path data utilized is gathered from Ref. [49] and tracks the eye of the storm at an interval of 6 hours, and рқ‘Үрқ‘ҹis
set to 7.25 days. Considering the tracking conditions, the number of targets (points along the storm path) for Hurricane
Sandy is рқ‘ғ= 29. Additionally, binary masking is applied to рқ‘үрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қfor each target in chronological order, such that target рқ‘қ
has a masking of one at time steps рқ‘ЎвҲҲ[1 + (рқ‘қвҲ’1)(рқ‘Ү/рқ‘ғ), рқ‘қ(рқ‘Ү/рқ‘ғ)] and a masking of zero otherwise. This masking
condition similarly applies for рқ‘үрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘қand рқ‘үв„“рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘қalong each stage in the time horizon. The same time step size, О”рқ‘Ў= 100 s,
is used in the case study, resulting in a total number of time steps of рқ‘Ү= 6264. Additionally, ground stations utilized by
disaster monitoring satellites and satellite orbits suitable for EO are employed. The ground stations are assigned as
two used by the Disaster Monitoring Constellation, the first being the Svalbard Satellite Station located in Sweden at
78.23 deg North and 15.41 deg East and the second being a satellite station located in Boecillo Spain at 41.54 deg North
and 4.70 deg West [51]. Furthermore, a Walker-delta constellation is used as it has become a common choice in recent
years for satellite configurations. The Walker-delta constellation for рқҗҫ= 4 satellites is defined at an altitude of 709 km
and takes the form 98.18 deg : 4/4/0, where the inclination is 98.18 deg, and four satellites are located in four orbital
planes with zero relative phasing between satellites. The constellation formation is selected to remain consistent with an
identical FOV case in Ref. [52]. The three-dimensional locations of Hurricane Sandy over time, the two selected ground
stations, and the initial satellite orbits are shown in Fig. 6b.
Secondly, additional orbital slot options that extend to changes in the orbital plane and phase are provided. Such
orbital slots allow equally spaced changes in inclination, RAAN, and phase, in which the plane-change slots extend in the
positive and negative direction of the initial condition. The resultant number of orbital slot options is рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ= рқ‘“(2рқ‘ҡвҲ’1),
where рқ‘“is the number of phasing options and рқ‘ҡis the number of plane-change options in each plane of either inclination
or RAAN. The case studies make use of рқ‘“= 15 and рқ‘ҡ= 5, resulting in рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ= 135, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK. A
demonstration of the orbital slot option space is shown in Fig. 8 of Ref. [38]. The maximum degree of separation
between the initial condition and the furthest plane-change option is determined through the use of rearranged boundary
value problems (assuming the entire budget, рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max, is consumed) from Ref. [46] with an applied scaling factor of 75 % to
29


--- Page 30 ---
20В°N
30В°N
Latitude
105В°W
 90В°W
 75В°W
 60В°W
Longitude
 500 mi 
 1000 km 
Hurricane Sandy
(a) Path of Hurricane Sandy
(b) Three-dimensional locations of Hurricane
Sandy, ground stations, and satellites
Fig. 6
Locations of case study parameters.
ensure the feasibility of multiple maneuvers.
As a result of the increase in orbital slot options, additional cost computation algorithms are employed for all
possible combinations of transfer types. This includes 1) phasing only, 2) inclination change only, 3) RAAN change
only, 4) simultaneous inclination and RAAN change, 5) inclination change followed by phasing, 6) RAAN change
followed by phasing, and 7) simultaneous inclination and RAAN change followed by phasing. As with phasing-only
computations, analytical algorithms from Ref. [46] are used. Finally, рқ‘Ҷ= 8 stages are used in the REOSSP-Exact and
the REOSSP-RHP. All other parameters and parameter generation methods are kept identical to those in Sec. V.A.
2. Case Study Results
The resultant schedules of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP are shown in Tables 4вҖ“6, respectively.
The tables depict the high-level schedule broken down by stage (in terms of the time in which a stage would occur for
the EOSSP), including the number of observations, downlink occurrences, and charging occurrences, as well as the
various orbital elements (inclination рқ‘–, RAAN О©, and argument of latitude рқ‘ў, with changed elements highlighted in
bold in the arrival stage) and the cost of orbital maneuvers for the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP. It should be
noted that Tables 5 and 6 report the values of рқ‘–, О©, and рқ‘ўbelonging to a specific orbital slot as defined at the start of the
schedule, although propagation with SGP4 accounts for perturbations throughout the schedule duration, including in the
generation of visibility and orbital maneuver cost parameters. Furthermore, the data and battery storage capacities over
time are shown for the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP in Figs. 7вҖ“9, respectively. The objective function
values of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP are рқ‘§рқҗё= 25, рқ‘§рқ‘…= 97, and ГҚрқ‘Ҷ
рқ‘ =1 рқ‘§RHP = 73, respectively,
while the figure of merit values for the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP are 0.80, 3.20, and 2.40 GB,
respectively. It should be noted that despite the relatively low amount of data gathered by the constellation within the
30


--- Page 31 ---
schedule horizon, the schedules only concern a single target (Hurricane Sandy) rather than any other passive monitoring
occurring. Additionally, the runtime of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and REOSSP-RHP are 0.24, 54.60, and 6.65 min,
respectively. As such, the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP improved upon the EOSSP in regards to the objective
function value by 288.00 % and 192.00 % respectively, and in regards to the figure of merit by 300.00 % and 200.00 %,
respectively. Separately, the REOSSP-RHP performed 24.74 % and 25.00 % worse than the REOSSP-Exact regarding
the objective function value and figure of merit, respectively. Meanwhile, the runtime of the REOSSP-RHP was only
12.18 % the runtime of the REOSSP-Exact, substantially improving computation time at a slight performance cost.

(b) EOSSP battery tracking.
Fig. 7
EOSSP schedule.
The optimal schedule of the EOSSP contains only at most four observations per satellite, with two satellites only
obtaining a single observation, which results in a low quantity of data and as such an objective function value of рқ‘§рқҗё= 25
and a figure of merit value of рқ‘Қрқҗё= 0.80 GB. Furthermore, since the objective is to balance the observation and downlink
of data, satellite 4 achieves two observations in stage eight while only downlinking one of these observations, resulting
in the satellite retaining some data that may be downlinked at a time later than the schedule horizon established. The
31


--- Page 32 ---
extra data retained by satellites 1 through 3 is a result of the previously mentioned discrepancy between the values рқҗ·obs
and рқҗ·comm, a difference of 2.50 MB extra in рқҗ·obs. The extra data remaining onboard each satellite is reflected in Fig. 3a
where satellite 4 retains 107.50 MB, satellite 2 retains 10.00 MB, and satellites 1 and 3 retain 2.50 MB. In addition, the
charging of each satellite is dominated by the battery constraints due to the small number of tasks performed in each
stage and the large amount of power obtained via charging, as shown in Fig. 7b. However, the large peaks shown are
not determined by an eclipse of sunlight, as any eclipse only lasts at most 35 min, slightly more than one-third of an
orbital period. The reason for the large peaks shown is the large variety of feasible charging opportunities due to the low
occurrence of an eclipse of sunlight, resulting in some charging schedules including the large peaks (such as satellites 2
and 4) while others do not (such as satellites 1 and 3).

The optimal schedule of the REOSSP-Exact performs significantly better than the EOSSP, by 288.00 % in terms
of the objective function value and 300.00 % in terms of the figure of merit, including at least eight observations per
satellite. The REOSSP-Exact also suffers from an observation with no subsequent downlink in stage eight by satellite 4.
As such, the extra data retained by each satellite is slightly higher than that retained by the satellites in the EOSSP as a

result of the higher quantity of observations. Specifically reflected in Fig. 8a, satellite 4 retains 120.00 MB, satellite 1
retains 22.50 MB, and satellites 2 and 3 retain 20.00 MB. Additionally, satellite 4 reaches the highest data storage value
of 415.00 MB within the fifth stage, wherein satellite 4 downlinks all data before the final stage (stage eight) at which
point two more observations are conducted with only a single downlink, which accounts for the extra data retained. The
charging schedule, shown in Fig. 8b, of the REOSSP-Exact may appear vastly different from the previous charging
schedule; however, this variation is a result of the large variety of feasible charging solutions as the longest eclipse for
this orbital maneuver pattern is similar to that of the EOSSP. Finally, the overall cost of the optimal REOSSP-Exact
schedule consumes 2.26 km/s, 75.48 % of the total budget provided, for a total of 15 transfers (of 32 possibilities for
transfers), of which four are a plane change (two of which are inclination raising and two of which are RAAN lowering).
Each satellite, listed in order 1 through 4, consumed 722.75 m/s, 713.15 m/s, 510.69 m/s, and 317.93 m/s, which is
96.37 %, 95.09 %, 68.09 %, and 42.39 % of each individual budget, respectively.
