22
34
Cost, m/s
441.25
0.00
0.00
0.00
0.00
16.37
0.00
291.96
downlink in stage eight, indicating that this phenomenon is a result of the visibility profiles of the target and ground
stations, wherein satellite four has visibility of a target near the end of the time horizon with no visibility of ground
stations afterward. Since the overall number of observations conducted by the REOSSP-RHP is less than that of the
REOSSP-Exact, the extra data retained by each satellite is 120.00, 17.50, 15.00, and 10.00 MB for satellites 4 through
1, respectively, as reflected in Fig. 9a. An additional phenomenon occurs in Fig. 9a, wherein data that is observed by
each satellite is downlinked to a ground station much sooner than in the REOSSP-Exact and EOSSP, a result of the
objective to downlink and observe being optimized for each group of two stages rather than over the accumulation of the
entire time horizon. A similar phenomenon occurs in Fig. 9b, wherein the charging of each satellite is maintained within
each subproblem, leading to a slightly more sporadic charging schedule when appended. Finally, the overall cost of the
optimal REOSSP-RHP schedule is substantially more expensive than that of the REOSSP-Exact, a result of the rather
small рқҗҝ= 1 stage lookahead incentivizing effective yet expensive maneuvers to occur at the start of the schedule. This is
displayed well in Table 6, where each satellite consumes on average 79.01 % of the maneuver budget within the first two
maneuvers. Overall, the optimal REOSSP-RHP schedule consumes 2.89 km/s, 96.43 % of the total budget provided,
34


--- Page 35 ---
for a total of 15 transfers (of 32 possibilities for transfers), of which four are a plane change (two of which are inclination
lowering, one of which is RAAN lowering, and one of which is RAAN raising). Each satellite, listed in order 1 through
4, consumed 745.76 m/s, 686.42 m/s, 711.10 m/s, and 749.58 m/s, which is 99.43 %, 91.52 %, 94.81 %, and 99.94 %
of each individual budget, respectively.
Overall, the highly adaptable nature of the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP enabled through reconfigurability,
a diverse set of orbital slots including plane and phase changes, and the reduction in computation time via the
REOSSP-RHP algorithm allows for performance much higher than that of the EOSSP. This effectiveness is shown
through the performance of significantly more observations and downlink occurrences by each satellite relative to the
EOSSP, while the REOSSP-RHP performs more expensive orbital maneuvers while completing computations much
faster. Additionally, all three scheduling algorithms return a resultant schedule that obeys all constraints, including
power consumption, charging, data management, and orbital maneuver budget limitations.
VI. Conclusions
This paper developed a mathematical formulation to integrate constellation reconfigurability into the EOSSP
through the use of MILP and previous research on the MCRP [26вҖ“28, 32, 33]. The REOSSP developed herein
optimizes the schedule of a cooperative satellite constellation capable of orbital maneuvering such that the number of
observations and subsequent data downlink occurrences is maximized, with a higher influence from the number of
downlink occurrences. Additionally, the REOSSP considers available data storage, battery storage, and a propellant
budget for orbital maneuvers, while scheduling the tasks to observe targets, downlink observed data, charge onboard
batteries, and perform constellation reconfiguration at predetermined stages. Furthermore, the REOSSP is solved via
the REOSSP-RHP, which significantly improves computational runtime compared to the REOSSP but at the cost of
yielding suboptimal solutions. The novel MILP formulation of the REOSSP and the REOSSP-RHP allows flexible
implementation, including the ability to include trajectory optimization for orbital maneuvers and optimal solutions
through commercial solvers. In conjunction, a baseline EOSSP is developed and utilized for the comparison.
The computational experiments conducted in Sec. V demonstrate the capability of the REOSSP-Exact to outperform
the EOSSP in a variety of scenarios, confirming the hypothesis set forth by previous comparative research [28, 32, 33].
Within the random instances, meant to demonstrate the flexibility of the framework under various parameters, the
average percent improvement of the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP over the EOSSP is 101.80 % and 78.06 %
in terms of the objective function value, respectively. When applied to Hurricane Sandy the REOSSP-Exact and
REOSSP-RHP exceed the performance of the EOSSP by 288.00 % and 192.00 % in terms of the objective function
value, respectively, and 300.00 % and 200.00 % in terms of the figure of merit, respectively. While the REOSSP-RHP
does not obtain as high-quality results as the REOSSP-Exact, in the event that the problem scale is sufficiently large so
as to cause the REOSSP-Exact to be unable to converge to a feasible solution, the REOSSP-RHP can obtain an optimal
35


--- Page 36 ---
solution to each subproblem while still significantly outperforming the EOSSP. Both the random instances and the
case study depict the performance increase provided by the REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP, while simultaneously
obeying all operational and physical constraints. Overall, the implementation of constellation reconfigurability provides
a greater throughput of observed data than the standard EOSSP.
Future research into the concept of constellation reconfigurability implemented in a scheduling format may take
many forms. Firstly, the scope of this paper is restricted to high-thrust orbital maneuver trajectory optimization,
whereas low-thrust maneuvers may allow for a lower operational cost of such a reconfigurable constellation. As
such, the implementation of various low-thrust trajectory optimization theories, such as those in Refs. [53, 54], may
result in an increased level of performance above the baseline with less expensive orbital maneuvers. Secondly, all
scheduling problems formulated in this paper assume deterministic characteristics, while many natural disasters or
other observable planetary phenomena are often stochastic. Therefore, the implementation of stochastic programming
similar to that in Ref. [55] to the scheduling problems developed in this paper may more accurately represent the
stochastic nature of natural disaster targets. Thirdly, the work in this paper focuses heavily on the REOSSP and
constellation reconfigurability as a whole, while previous work in Refs. [32, 33] additionally considers satellite agility,
thus contributing to the potential of a similar AEOSSP formulation to be developed and used for further benchmarking
of satellite agility against constellation reconfigurability. Finally, the further comparison of a small constellation of
maneuverable satellites against a much larger fixed constellation of either nadir-directional or agile satellites may
additionally prove insightful.
Appendix A: Proof of Formulation Equivalence with No Orbital Maneuvers
The EOSSP and REOSSP are based on the same principles, such that the only differences lie within the orbital
maneuverability, wherein the REOSSP contributes the properties of the currently occupied orbital slots. Such properties
include the visibility of task priorities and the battery required to perform orbital maneuvers, рқҗөrecon.
Theorem 1: Let рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max = 0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK in the REOSSP, then рқ‘§рқҗё= рқ‘§рқ‘….
Proof: Suppose that for the EOSSP and REOSSP, all constant parameters such as О”рқ‘Ўor рқҗҫ, are identical. Note that
each stage aligns sequentially such that рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ of the REOSSP corresponds to рқ‘Ў+ (рқ‘ вҲ’1)рқ‘Үрқ‘ вҲҲT of the EOSSP, as
reflected in Fig. 4. This sequential alignment also applies to the visibility parameters рқ‘үрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘қ, рқҗәрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—рқ‘”, and рқҗ»рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘—. Additionally,
the initial orbital positions of each satellite (those applied to the EOSSP) apply to a single orbital slot option index рқ‘—.
Finally, the number of stages рқ‘Ҷand the number of orbital slots рқҗҪрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK may remain generic.
Firstly, constraints (10c) restrict the orbital maneuvers of satellites to those with a cost рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—whose accumulation
does not exceed рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max. Since рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max = 0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, only the maneuvers from orbital slot рқ‘–вҲҲJ рқ‘ вҲ’1,рқ‘ҳto orbital slot рқ‘—вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
where рқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—= 0, which only occurs if рқ‘–= рқ‘—, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0} by definition, are feasible. Furthermore, at stage рқ‘ = 1,
the orbital slot рқ‘–вҲҲJ 0рқ‘ҳis a singleton set of the initial orbital positions for each satellite (those applied to the EOSSP).
36


--- Page 37 ---
As such, the satellites of the EOSSP and REOSSP are located in the same orbital slot throughout the entire time
horizon. Finally, the assignment рқ‘–вҲҲJ 0рқ‘ҳ, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK and рқ‘—= рқ‘–, рқ‘—вҲҲJ рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0} obeys constraints (10a)
and (10b) since only orbital slot рқ‘–is occupied in all stages рқ‘ вҲҲS such that рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘–= 1, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0} and
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘–рқ‘—= 0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, рқ‘—вү рқ‘–. Since each satellite cannot perform an orbital maneuver, it can be assumed that
рқҗөrecon = 0, thus eliminating the possible difference in battery power consumption.
Secondly, constraints (11aвҖ“11c) assign the visibility of targets, ground stations, and the sun, respectively. Since
рқ‘–вҲҲJ 0рқ‘ҳ, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK is the orbital position applied to the EOSSP, рқ‘–= рқ‘—, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0}, and the visibility parameters
align sequentially, the visibility of targets, ground stations, and the sun are identically assigned to the EOSSP and
REOSSP. Furthermore, the VTWs for the tasks of target observation, data downlink to ground stations, and solar
charging are also identically assigned. Additionally, since objective function (2) of the EOSSP and objective function (8)
of the REOSSP are functionally equivalent, and the VTWs of each task are identical, the assignment of tasks is
subsequently identical. In a similar manner to the assignment of orbital slots, constraints (11d) are inherently obeyed as
the identical task assignment obeys constraints (4d).
Thirdly, constraints (12) track and restrict the data storage of each satellite to within the bounds рқҗ·min and рқҗ·max
dependent upon the occurrence of target observation and data downlink to ground stations. Since the values of рқҗ·comm
and рқҗ·obs are constant, each stage is sequentially aligned, and task occurrence is identical between the EOSSP and
REOSSP, constraints (12c) and (12d) are inherently identical to constraints (5b) and (5c), respectively, and are thusly
obeyed. Additionally, constraints (12a) and (12b) contribute the current time step task performance to the subsequent
time step data storage value, wherein constraints (12a) apply within each stage and constraints (12b) apply between each
stage. As such, constraints (12a) and (12b) operate identically to constraints (5a).
Fourthly, constraints (13) and (14) track and restrict the battery storage, respectively, of each satellite to within
the bounds рқҗөmin and рқҗөmax dependent upon task performance; the consideration of orbital maneuvers may be removed
through the assumption that рқҗөrecon = 0. Since the values of рқҗөcomm, рқҗөcharge, рқҗөobs, and рқҗөtime are constant, each stage
is sequentially aligned, and task occurrence is identical, constraints (14a) are inherently identical to constraints (6b)
and are also obeyed. As an aside, since рқҗөrecon = 0, constraints (14d) simplify to рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max вүҘ0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK which is implied,
and constraints (14c) simplify to рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ вҲ’ГҚ
рқ‘қвҲҲP рқҗөobsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘қвҲ’ГҚ
рқ‘”вҲҲG рқҗөcommрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘”вҲ’рқҗөtime вүҘ0, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0, рқ‘Ҷ}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK.
With these simplifications, constraints (14b) and (14c) function similarly to constraints (12a) and (12b) in that
both contribute the current time step task performance to the subsequent time step battery storage value within
and between each stage, respectively. As such, constraints (14b) and (14c) operate identically to constraints (6c).
Furthermore, constraints (13c) simplify to рқ‘Ҹ1рқ‘ҳ
1 = рқҗөрқ‘ҳ
max, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK, an assumption of the EOSSP, and constraints (13b)
simplify to рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ +1,рқ‘ҳ
1
= рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ + рқҗөchargeв„Һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ вҲ’ГҚ
рқ‘қвҲҲP рқҗөobsрқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘қвҲ’ГҚ
рқ‘”вҲҲG рқҗөcommрқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘”вҲ’рқҗөtime, вҲҖрқ‘ вҲҲS \ {0, рқ‘Ҷ}, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK.
Therefore, constraints (13a) and (13b) function similarly to constraints (12a) and (12b), and thus operate identically to
constraints (6a).
37


--- Page 38 ---
Finally, objective functions (8) and (2) compute the objective function values рқ‘§рқ‘…and рқ‘§рқҗё, respectively. Since task
occurrence is identical and each stage is sequentially aligned, the values of рқ‘һрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”and рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қwill be the same as the values of
рқ‘һрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘”and рқ‘Ұрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ
рқ‘Ўрқ‘қfor similar time steps рқ‘Ў+ (рқ‘ вҲ’1)рқ‘Үрқ‘ вҲҲT and рқ‘ЎвҲҲT рқ‘ , respectively. As such, since the value of рқҗ¶is constant,
the objective function values рқ‘§рқҗё= рқ‘§рқ‘…. Therefore, the EOSSP and REOSSP return identical schedules with identical
objective function values under conditions wherein рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max = 0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK.
в–Ў
To further validate the above proof numerically, the case study in Sec. V.B is repeated with the parameter
рқ‘җрқ‘ҳ
max = 0, вҲҖрқ‘ҳвҲҲK. The results follow that рқ‘§рқҗё= рқ‘§рқ‘…= 25 since the satellites of the REOSSP were forced to remain in
the initial condition orbital slots for the entire duration of the schedule horizon. As such, the above proof is validated
using realistic target data and the same parameters and parameter generation methods as the case study in Sec. V.B.
Appendix B: Additional Random Instance Results Statistics
In addition to the statistical information provided by the box charts in Sec. V.A, the percent improvement of the
REOSSP-Exact and REOSSP-RHP over the EOSSP, as well as the runtime of the EOSSP, REOSSP-Exact, and
REOSSP-RHP, in each instance, is shown as bar charts in Figs. 10a and Fig. 10b, respectively. The ordering of Figs. 10a
and 10b is in ascending order of the REOSSP-RHP metric from left to right.

Acknowledgments

improved the quality of the manuscript. The authors also thank the Department of Mechanical, Materials and Aerospace
Engineering at West Virginia University for providing the support to publish this manuscript as open access.
